assesment:
Basal Cell Carcinoma (Superficial)
Considering the patient's history, examination and histopathology, the diagnosis is most consistent with Basal Cell Carcinoma.
Management options discussed with patient: Mohs micrographic surgery, ED&C, excisional surgery, and cryosurgery. Risk and benefits of each option reviewed.
Patient opted to proceed with the following treatment: Mohs done today. Patient tolerated well. Wound care reviewed and instructions given to the patient. Patient will apply Mupirocin to affected area BID for 5-7 days. RTO in 7-10 days to remove sutures and check healing.
Follow up: after 7-10 Days for Suture removal with BABAR RAO, MD
mohsNotes:
Location: Left Dorsal Foot
Pre-op Diagnosis: Basal Cell Carcinoma Superficial
Pathology Reference: HD23-9668
Mohs Log: 23-37
Allergies: No Known Allergies, 
In an informative discussion of the mohs procedure, the nature of the skin cancer, 
 alternative treatment options, consequences of no treatment and convalescence were reviewed with the patient. 
 The risks and benefits, including but not limited to: bleeding, spread of infection, pain, scarring, bruising, 
 distortion of the surrounding areas, allergic reaction to the medications used, recurrence of the tumor, 
 and opening of the wound were discussed and understood by the patient. 
All questions from the patient were answered adequately. 
 No guarantees were given and the patient was informed that Mohs surgery is indicated to provide 
 the highest cure rate by excising the tissue in multiple stages and preserving the healthy tissue as much as possible. 
After obtaining informed consent, the patient was given a mirror and 
 corroborated the location of the tumor prior to surgery. 
 Mohs Micrographic Procedure:
Attention was turned toward the surgical site, which was disinfected and cleansed with hibiclens. The patient was draped using a sterile technique. Local anesthetic lidocaine 1 % with epinephrine was infiltrated around the site. The procedure was performed in accordance with the Mohs micrographic surgical technique. 
 The tumor site was debulk prior to the first Mohs stage. With each stage, a thin layer of tissue was excised with a narrow margin of normal appearing skin using the Mohs fresh tissue technique. Hemostasis was achieved with Pressure. The surgical site was dressed with gauze pressure dressings and the patient remained in the surgical area. The tissue was prepared for the cryostat and sectioned. Each section was coded, cut and stained for microscopic examination. The slides were then analyzed by the Mohs surgeon for any evidence of remaining tumor. Areas noted to be positive on the previous stage (if applicable) were removed with the mohs technique and processed for analysis. This process was repeated until a tumor free plane was obtained. 
1st Stage: 2 Sections, Negative
Post-Mohs Deficit Size: 1.5 x 1.8 cm.
 Repair Options:
 
 After a discussion of wound management options, 
 due to the size and location of the defect, and the need to achieve optimal cosmesis, 
 avoid hematoma, the following wound management was agreed upon to provide the best functional and cosmetic result. 
 Performed: 
 The wound was closed with an Adjacent Tissue Transfer on the same day of mohs. 
 Reconstruction:
Repair: Adjacent Tissue Transfer - Advanced 
The postoperative deficit was 1.5 x 1.8 cm. Additional relaxing incisions were made along the wound to develop pedicle skin flaps which were then widely undermined and advanced to close the deficit. The defect was undermined extensively (3.1 cm) to reduce tension and distortion of the adjacent structures. Buried absorbable sutures were also used to close the subcutaneous and dermal components of the defect. The wound was approximated utilizing 4-O Vicryl and 4-O Nylon sutures. 
The final closure size is 4.6 cm2. 
 Post-Operative:
The patient tolerated the procedure well with no complications, and minimal bleeding. The wound was covered with Vaseline and Press Dressing. Detailed oral and written post-operative instructions were reviewed and given to the patient. The patient instructed to remove dressing in 24 hours, sleep with the surgical site elevated this evening, avoid alcohol, trauma and tobacco.
 Patient was informed to return in 10 Days.
examination: - well healed scar at site of previous biopsy located on the left foot.

assesment:
Basal Cell Carcinoma Pathology Left cheek - (WC23-00516) 
Considering the patient's history, examination and histopathology, the diagnosis is most consistent with Basal Cell Carcinoma.
Management options discussed with patient: Mohs micrographic surgery, ED&C, excisional surgery, and cryosurgery. Risk and benefits of each option reviewed.
Patient opted to proceed with the following treatment: MOHS today. Two stages preformed due to patients age, and medical health condition site was unable to be cleared. Re-occurrence of lesion will proceed with DM with ED&C. Explained this to patient, and patients son Brendan. Both acknowledged understanding.
Mohs done today. Discussed the procedure with the patient and answered all of their questions. The pathology report and previous biopsy photos were reviewed and the site was confirmed with the patient. Patient verbalized understanding of the risks of surgery, including infection and scarring. Patient opted to proceed with the procedure. Patient instructed to monitor for signs of infection, including swelling, erythema, wound drainage or fever.
Basal Cell Carcinoma Pathology Mid Upper Lip - (WC23-00517) 
Considering the patient's history, examination and histopathology, the diagnosis is most consistent with Basal Cell Carcinoma.
Management options discussed with patient: Mohs micrographic surgery, ED&C, excisional surgery, and cryosurgery. Risk and benefits of each option reviewed.
Patient opted to proceed with the following treatment: MOHS today. Start topical Mupirocin 2 % topical ointment apply to affected area as directed. Start Doxycycline 100mg take 1 tab po daily x 7 days. Doxycycline is generally well-tolerated. It was advised to take the medication with meals, and to stay upright for one hour after taking the medication. SPF was recommended given increased risk of photosensitivity. The most common side effects are diarrhea or loose stools, nausea, abdominal pain, and vomiting. Yeast infections may be more common- recommend probiotic or live active cultures in yogurt. Informed the patient to d/c drug immediately if they develop rash, joint pain, fevers
Follow up: after 1 Week for Suture removal with Babar Rao, MD
currentmedication: Centany - (2 % topical ointment - 45 Gram - apply three times a day on wounds for 14 days)

assesment:
Squamous Cell Carcinoma - Left Jaw (HC23-01432)
Considering the patient's history, examination and histopathology, the diagnosis is most consistent with Squamous Cell Carcinoma.
Management options discussed with patient: Mohs micrographic surgery, ED&C, excisional surgery, and cryosurgery. Risk and benefits of each option reviewed.
Patient opted to proceed with the following treatment:
- Start topical MUPIROCIN - 2 % topical ointment - 30 Gram - Apply a thin film to affected area three times daily
-Mohs done today. Discussed the procedure with the patient and her daughter and answered all of their questions. The pathology report and previous biopsy photos were reviewed and the site was confirmed with the patient. Patient verbalized understanding of the risks of surgery, including infection and scarring. Patient opted to proceed with the procedure. Patient instructed to monitor for signs of infection, including swelling, erythema, wound drainage or fever.
-Had an informative discussion about the Mohs procedure with the patient. Reviewed with patient that Mohs surgery is a procedure to treat skin cancer that involves cutting away thin layers of the skin while sacrificing a minimum amount of normal tissue. Mohs surgery is ideal for skin cancers that have a high risk of recurrence, for areas where one would want to keep as much healthy tissue as possible, for tumors that have edges that are hard to define or are large and grow quickly. Risks of Mohs surgery include but are not limited to: bleeding, pain or tenderness at site, and possible risk of infection and large scarring. Other uncommon risks are temporary or permanent numbness and/or weakness of the surgical area.
After MOHS procedure was finalized the patient reported numbness on the surgery side and she was having trouble speaking. Due to patient's advanced age and medical history of hypertension, proceed to call 911. When the paramedics arrived they took the BP and stated it was over the 200's. Patient was taken to Saint Agnes Hospital for further management to r/o stroke. 
Follow up: after 10 Days for Suture removal with Sarah Medina, NP

Atorvastatin 40mg
Lisinopril 40mg
Metoprolol 50mg
Aspirin 81mg
Norvaso 25mg
Vitamins
Calcium
Xalaton
Dorzolamide-Timolol

currentmedication: amlodipine besylate 10mg, atenolol 50mg, atorvastatin 80mg, ibuprofen, losartan, gabapentin 600mg, aspirin 81mg-Spironlactone 25mg, Clopidogrel 75mg, Vitamin C, Fish oil.
biopsyNotes:
A. Excision Malignant Lesion & Margins
Clinical Diagnosis: Excised Malignant Melanoma HC25-00396
Location: Right Ant Neck
Method: Excision
Lesion Size: 1 - 1 cm
Excision Size, including margins: 2 x 2 cm
The patient was evaluated preoperative and noted to be in stable condition. 
 Informed consent was obtained from the patient. All risks, benefits, and alternatives regarding the survey have been reviewed in detail with the patient. 
 This includes risks of bleeding, infection, scarring recurrence of lesion and need for further procedures. 
After consent was obtained, attention was turned toward the area of interest which was disinfected and cleaned with alcohol. Local anesthetic, 2% lidocaine with epinephrine was infiltrated around the site. An elliptical incision with the appropriate margins was made using a Number 15 to obtain a full thickness specimen. Dissection of the surrounding/overlying tissue was performed to isolate, expose and remove the lesion. The specimen was placed in a bottle labeled A. #WC25-509. 
Repair: Complex Closure 
The wound was closed with a complex closure on 04-09-2025. The wound size with margins is 2 - 2 cm. 
Complex repair was performed because the defect extended into of the deep subcutaneous tissue and superficial non-muscular fascia. Extensive wide undermining was performed to a distance of 2 cm (defect width perpendicular to closure is 2 cm) along the edge of the entire surgical defect to mobilize adjacent tissue, reduce wound tension and minimize the distortion of the adjacent functional structure.
Buried, absorbable retention sutures were placed to close the deep subcutaneous tissue and superficial non-muscular fascia. Retention suture placement was necessary: to avoid deforming, a depressed and contracted scar, anatomic distortion and functional deficit in adjacent fixed structures, injury to tendons and nerves in the area, to ensure proper range of motion, maintain function in the area, preserve the normal anatomy, reduce subcutaneous dead space to reduce the risk of hematoma, to reduce tension to skin to allow closure, and to reduce the risk of necrosis, infection and wound dehiscence.
The deep subcutaneous tissue, superficial non-muscular fascia and dermis was closed with 4-O Vicryl sutures followed by meticulous epidermal approximation.Epidermal closure was achieved with 4-O Nylon sutures.
Closure necessitated advanced layered techniques beyond a routine intermediate repair due to the defect size, depth, and location. The final closure size was 4.2 cm.
Pressure was applied to achieve hemostasis. Vaseline was applied at the wound site and it was bandaged. Patient tolerated the procedure well and wound care instructions were reviewed and given to the patient.
mohsNotes:
Location: Right Post Lateral Neck
Pre-op Diagnosis: Squamous Cell Carcinoma 
Pathology Reference: HC25-00393
Mohs Log: 6113
Current Medication: Asprin
In an informative discussion of the mohs procedure, the nature of the skin cancer, 
 alternative treatment options, consequences of no treatment and convalescence were reviewed with the patient. 
 The risks and benefits, including but not limited to: bleeding, spread of infection, pain, scarring, bruising, 
 distortion of the surrounding areas, allergic reaction to the medications used, recurrence of the tumor, 
 and opening of the wound were discussed and understood by the patient. 
All questions from the patient were answered adequately. 
 No guarantees were given and the patient was informed that Mohs surgery is indicated to provide 
 the highest cure rate by excising the tissue in multiple stages and preserving the healthy tissue as much as possible. 
After obtaining informed consent, the patient was given a mirror and 
 corroborated the location of the tumor prior to surgery. 
 Mohs Micrographic Procedure:
Attention was turned toward the surgical site, which was disinfected and cleansed with alcohol. The patient was draped using a sterile technique. Local anesthetic lidocaine 1 % with epinephrine was infiltrated around the site. The procedure was performed in accordance with the Mohs micrographic surgical technique. 
 The tumor site was debulk prior to the first Mohs stage. With each stage, a thin layer of tissue was excised with a narrow margin of normal appearing skin using the Mohs fresh tissue technique. Hemostasis was achieved with Pressure, Electrocautery. The surgical site was dressed with gauze pressure dressings and the patient remained in the surgical area. The tissue was prepared for the cryostat and sectioned. Each section was coded, cut and stained for microscopic examination. The slides were then analyzed by the Mohs surgeon for any evidence of remaining tumor. Areas noted to be positive on the previous stage (if applicable) were removed with the mohs technique and processed for analysis. This process was repeated until a tumor free plane was obtained. 
1st Stage: 2 Sections, Positive
2nd Stage: 2 Sections, Positive
3rd Stage: 1 Sections, Negative
Post-Mohs Deficit Size: 2.3 x 3.1 cm.
 Repair Options:
 
 After a discussion of wound management options, 
 due to the size and location of the defect, and the need to achieve optimal cosmesis, 
 avoid hematoma, the following wound management was agreed upon to provide the best functional and cosmetic result. 
 Performed: 
 The wound was closed with an Adjacent Tissue Transfer on the same day of mohs. 
 Reconstruction:
Repair: Adjacent Tissue Transfer - Advanced 
The postoperative deficit was 2.3 x 3.1 cm. Additional relaxing incisions were made along the wound to develop pedicle skin flaps which were then widely undermined and advanced to close the deficit. The defect extended into the deep subcutaneous tissue and superficial fascia. The wound was undermined extensively (1 cm) to mobilize adjacent tissue, reduce wound tension and minimize the distortion of the adjacent functional structures. Buried absorbable sutures were placed to close the subcutaneous tissue and superficial fascia, followed by meticulous epidermal approximation. Closure necessitated advanced layered techniques beyond a routine intermediate repair due to the defect size, depth, and location. The wound was approximated utilizing 4-O Vicryl and 4-O Nylon sutures. 
The final closure size is 6.5 cm2. 
 Post-Operative:
The patient tolerated the procedure well with no complications, and minimal bleeding. The wound was covered with Vaseline and Press Dressing. Detailed oral and written post-operative instructions were reviewed and given to the patient. The patient instructed to remove dressing in 24 hours, sleep with the surgical site elevated this evening, avoid alcohol, trauma and tobacco.
 Patient was informed to return in 10 Days.

assesment:
Squamous Cell Carcinoma FOCAL - Mid Forehead (WC25-01708)
Considering the patients history, examination and histopathology, the diagnosis is most consistent with Squamous Cell Carcinoma.
Management options discussed with patient: Mohs micrographic surgery, ED&C, excisional surgery, and cryosurgery. Risk and benefits of each option reviewed.
Patient opted to proceed with the following treatment:
 Mohs done today. Discussed the procedure with the patient and answered all of their questions. The pathology report and previous biopsy photos were reviewed and the site was confirmed with the patient. Patient verbalized understanding of the risks of surgery, including infection and scarring. Patient opted to proceed with the procedure. Patient instructed to monitor for signs of infection, including swelling, erythema, wound drainage or fever.
 
-Had an informative discussion about the Mohs procedure with the patient. Reviewed with patient that Mohs surgery is a procedure to treat skin cancer that involves cutting away thin layers of the skin while sacrificing a minimum amount of normal tissue. Mohs surgery is ideal for skin cancers that have a high risk of recurrence, for areas where one would want to keep as much healthy tissue as possible, for tumors that have edges that are hard to define or are large and grow quickly. Risks of Mohs surgery include but are not limited to: bleeding, pain or tenderness at site, and possible risk of infection and large scarring. Other uncommon risks are temporary or permanent numbness and/or weakness of the surgical area.
- Start topical Mupirocin 2% ointment apply to affected area TID. 
Follow up: after 10 Days for Suture removal with Babar Rao, MD
